english: Korean marinated spicy chicken over rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Korean spicy marinated chicken with rice; mapped to broader 'chicken dish' concept as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Korean spicy chicken rice bowl; A0CKM (chicken meat dish) used as the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for Korean spicy chicken with rice as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 150 g of spicy marinated chicken and 150 g of cooked white rice, as typically plated in a Korean casual dining or hawker context.

nutrient_source.title: Korean Spicy Chicken (with Rice) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Korean spicy chicken (with rice)', CR ID F05100070. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; White rice GI reference (Jasmine/polished white rice, GI ~65–72)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean spicy chicken with rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 65 based on the dominant carbohydrate component — cooked white rice — which has a well-established GI of 65–72 in the University of Sydney GI Database. The chicken and gochujang marinade components contribute negligible carbohydrate and are expected to modestly lower the composite GI through protein and fat co-ingestion. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 51.72) / 100 ≈ 33.6, rounded to 33.5, based on 300 g serving with 17.24 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 146 mg per 100 g — below the 200 mg/100 g threshold for moderate concern
hypertension.guidance: At 146 mg sodium per 100 g, this dish is within acceptable limits for hypertensive individuals when consumed as a standard 300 g serving (~438 mg sodium), but patients should avoid adding soy sauce, kimchi, or other high-sodium condiments, and should monitor cumulative daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load (GL ≈ 33.5 per serving) driven by white rice with low dietary fibre (0.92 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base delivers a high glycaemic load per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute brown rice or mixed-grain rice to reduce postprandial glucose excursion, increase vegetable portions to add fibre, and limit serving size to no more than 150–200 g total.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate sodium (146 mg/100 g) and unknown saturated fat content from chicken skin and marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish provides moderate energy (124 kcal/100 g) and relatively low fat (2.74 g/100 g), which is broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet, but saturated fat data are unavailable from the HPB source; patients with CVD should request skinless chicken preparation and avoid high-sodium accompaniments to keep total sodium within the 1500–2000 mg/day cardiovascular guideline.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Gochujang contains chili (nightshade family); white rice provides refined carbohydrates that may promote glycaemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients with solanine sensitivity should avoid or limit this dish due to chili content in gochujang; substituting brown rice and requesting reduced chili marinade can lower both glycaemic and nightshade-related inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Korean Spicy Chicken with Rice (양념 치킨 덮밥, Yangnyeom Chikin Deopbap) is a Korean-style dish consisting of chicken pieces marinated in a spicy-sweet sauce based on gochujang (Korean fermented chili paste), soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and sesame oil, served over a bed of steamed white rice. The chicken is typically stir-fried or pan-cooked until caramelised, yielding a sticky, spicy, and slightly sweet flavour profile with a tender texture. The dish is a popular Korean comfort food and is widely available in Korean restaurants and hawker-style food courts across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 124 kcal, 7.36 g protein, 2.74 g fat, and 17.24 g carbohydrate, with 0.92 g dietary fibre and 146 mg sodium. The relatively low fat content reflects the lean chicken and rice base, while the moderate sodium level is diluted by the rice component. Clinically, the dish carries a high glycaemic load per standard serving due to white rice, making it a concern for Type 2 Diabetes management; substitution with brown or mixed-grain rice is recommended. Sodium is within acceptable limits for hypertension at standard portions. The gochujang marinade contains chili, a nightshade ingredient, which may be relevant for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and solanine sensitivity. The dish is halal-suitable and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05100070) at confidence 0.95. The record is a canonical HPB entry with reviewed provenance. GI is an analogous estimate based on the white rice component from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the standard approach for composite rice-based dishes without a direct GI measurement.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this composite dish as of 2026. Calcium (28.0 mg/100 g) and iron (0.77 mg/100 g) are available from the HPB source and have been populated. GI value of 65 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Glycaemic load per serving calculated at 300 g serving size. The input record listed gi_value as 0 with confidence 0.328 from 'Embedded GI Database' — this has been replaced with the approved University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate. The input health_suitability condition key 'diabetes' has been mapped to the required 'type2_diabetes' key with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas scoring rules.
